exercises.translation: This handwriting is hard to read.

wrong_choice_explanations.やすい: やすい means 'easy to do' — it's the opposite of the difficulty being expressed.
wrong_choice_explanations.たい: たい expresses desire 'want to read' — it doesn't describe the handwriting's legibility.
wrong_choice_explanations.ている: ている indicates ongoing action — it doesn't express difficulty in reading.

exercises.example_sentence: このナイフは切れ__から、新しいのを買いました。
exercises.translation: This knife is hard to cut with, so I bought a new one.

wrong_choice_explanations.やすい: やすい means 'easy to cut' — if it cut easily, there'd be no need for a new knife.
wrong_choice_explanations.たい: たい means 'want to cut' — this doesn't explain why a new knife was necessary.
wrong_choice_explanations.ません: ません is negative polite form — it doesn't grammatically fit this sentence structure.

exercises.example_sentence: 新しい単語は覚え__くて、困っています。
exercises.translation: New vocabulary is hard to remember, so I'm having trouble.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would make it 'easy to remember' — this contradicts having trouble with it.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to remember — it doesn't describe the inherent difficulty.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express difficulty in remembering.

exercises.example_sentence: この機械は使い__そうですね。
exercises.translation: This machine looks hard to use.

wrong_choice_explanations.やすい: やすい would mean 'easy to use' — this doesn't match the concerned tone of the statement.
wrong_choice_explanations.たい: たい expresses wanting to use — it doesn't describe the machine's apparent complexity.
wrong_choice_explanations.ます: ます is polite verb ending — it doesn't form a proper adjective describing difficulty.

exercises.example_sentence: 雨の日は洗濯物が乾き__いです。
exercises.translation: On rainy days, laundry is hard to dry.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would mean 'easy to dry' — this contradicts the challenge posed by rainy weather.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to dry — it doesn't describe the inherent drying difficulty on rainy days.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express difficulty in drying.

exercises.example_sentence: この靴は履き__から、あまり履きません。
exercises.translation: These shoes are hard to put on, so I don't wear them much.

wrong_choice_explanations.やすい: やすい means 'easy to put on' — if they were easy, the person would wear them more often.
wrong_choice_explanations.たい: たい means 'want to put on' — this doesn't explain the infrequent wearing.
wrong_choice_explanations.ました: ました is past tense — it doesn't describe the inherent difficulty of putting on the shoes.

exercises.example_sentence: 彼の説明は分かり__くて、質問しました。
exercises.translation: His explanation was hard to understand, so I asked questions.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would make it 'easy to understand' — then there'd be no need to ask questions.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to understand — it doesn't describe the explanation's clarity.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express difficulty in understanding.

exercises.example_sentence: この魚は食べ__い骨が多いです。
exercises.translation: This fish has many bones that are hard to eat around.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would mean the bones are 'easy to eat around' — this contradicts the problem being described.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to eat — it doesn't describe the difficulty caused by the bones.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express eating difficulty.

exercises.example_sentence: 古い窓は開け__いので、修理が必要です。
exercises.translation: The old window is hard to open, so it needs repair.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would mean 'easy to open' — then repair wouldn't be necessary.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to open — it doesn't describe the mechanical difficulty.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express difficulty in opening.

exercises.example_sentence: この問題は解き__かったので、先生に聞きました。
exercises.translation: This problem was hard to solve, so I asked the teacher.

wrong_choice_explanations.やす: やす would make it 'was easy to solve' — then asking the teacher wouldn't be necessary.
wrong_choice_explanations.たく: たく relates to wanting to solve — it doesn't describe the problem's inherent difficulty.
wrong_choice_explanations.てい: てい is part of progressive form — it doesn't express solving difficulty.
